nativeRange.habitat: Wet soils, shallow waters, marshes, rice paddies, seasonally flooded wetlands, and slow-flowing streams. Most aquarium species come from soft, acidic, mineral-poor waters of South and South-East Asia.

decorPreferences: open space around rosette for growth

summary: Eriocaulon is a genus of rosette plants known for their distinctive star-shaped form and demanding care requirements. Aquarium species are slow-growing and intolerant of hard or alkaline water, requiring soft acidic conditions, strong lighting, CO2 injection, and a nutrient-rich substrate.
careNotes:
Eriocaulon is a large genus of roughly 500 species with nearly global distribution, but only a handful are regularly kept in the aquarium hobby. The species most commonly available in the trade include E. cinereum, E. quinquangulare, E. setaceum, E. ratnagiricum, E. polaris, and E. parkeri. Adult size varies considerably across the genus: E. cinereum stays compact at around 8 cm and works well as a foreground or midground accent, while E. setaceum can reach 30 cm and is used in background plantings, where its fine, grass-like leaves resemble hairgrass at scale.

All Eriocaulon species kept in aquariums rely heavily on root uptake, making a rich, fine-grained aquasoil substrate essential — coarse gravel or inert sand will not sustain them. Supplemental fertilization through iron-rich root tabs is strongly recommended, and regular column dosing with macronutrients (nitrate 10–50 mg/L, phosphate 0.1–3 mg/L, potassium) and micronutrients, particularly iron and trace elements, is also considered necessary for sustained growth. Pressurized CO2 injection is generally regarded as essential rather than optional; without it, most specimens stagnate or decline.

Eriocaulon is notoriously sensitive to parameter swings. Sudden changes in pH, temperature, or substrate disturbance can trigger leaf melt and rosette collapse. Plants started from tissue culture tend to transition to submerged growth more reliably than emersed-grown nursery stock, which often melts back significantly before establishing. Hard, alkaline tap water — generally any supply with a gH or kH above 8 — is incompatible with most species; water purified by reverse osmosis and remineralized to soft, acidic parameters is strongly recommended.
breedingNotes: Most Eriocaulon species propagate vegetatively via splitting the mother rosette into daughter plants once side shoots develop at the base, or by cutting off naturally produced daughter plants. Emersed-grown plants flower readily and produce small wind-pollinated flowers on tall stalks; seed propagation is possible but uncommon in the aquarium hobby and slow.

primary.name: Flowgrow (Eriocaulon cinereum)
primary.notes: Primary data source for water parameters (temp 18-30 °C, pH 4.5-7, kH 0-7), lighting (high), CO2 (25-40 mg/L), growth rate (slow), placement (foreground/midground/nano), max height (~8 cm), care level (difficult), native range (South-East Asia rice paddies; widespread Africa, Asia, Australia), and propagation (seeds, splitting, daughter plants). Used as anchor because Buce Plant's E. cinereum product page returned HTTP 404.

additional.name: Wikipedia (Eriocaulon)
additional.notes: Used for taxonomy (family Eriocaulaceae, order Poales), genus species count (~500 species, 496 accepted as of May 2026), global distribution (Americas, British Isles, sub-Saharan Africa, central/east/south/south-east Asia), morphology (rosette herbs resembling Cyperaceae/Juncaceae), habitat (wet soils, shallow water, wetlands, flatwoods), and ecology ('weak competitors compared to many other wetland plant species'). No aquarium-specific data.

additional.name: Flowgrow (Eriocaulon setaceum)
additional.notes: Cross-checked genus husbandry on a second species: temp 20-30 °C, pH 4.5-7, kH 0-7, lighting high (0.75+ watts/L), CO2 25-40 mg/L (required), growth medium, placement background/midground, care difficult, native South-East Asia and Australia. Confirmed iron and trace-element fertilization, nitrate 10-50 mg/L, phosphate 0.1-3 mg/L. Propagation by cuttings up to 5 cm; plant 'does not respond well to trimming'.
